name: Pizzagate Full
description: Pizzagate is a debunked conspiracy theory that originated during the 2016 United States presidential election. It falsely claimed that prominent Democratic politicians and high-ranking government officials were running a child sex trafficking ring operating out of the Comet Ping Pong pizzeria in Washington, D.C. The theory began on social media platforms like 4chan and Reddit, fueled by misinterpreted emails from the personal account of Hillary Clinton's campaign chairman, John Podesta, which were leaked to the public. Proponents of Pizzagate interpreted coded language within these emails as evidence of child abuse and human trafficking. They falsely linked Comet Ping Pong and its owner, James Alefantis, to a network of pedophiles including Hillary Clinton, Barack Obama, and other prominent Democrats. The theory quickly spread online, leading to harassment and death threats against Comet Ping Pong's staff and owner. In December 2016, Edgar Maddison Welch traveled from North Carolina to Comet Ping Pong and fired an AR-15 rifle inside the restaurant, intending to “investigate” the conspiracy for himself. He was later arrested and sentenced to four years in prison. Mainstream media outlets and law enforcement agencies thoroughly investigated the claims and found no evidence to support them. The emails were misinterpreted and taken out of context, and there was no evidence of a child sex trafficking ring operating at Comet Ping Pong or any other location linked to the conspiracy. Pizzagate is widely considered a dangerous example of disinformation and the power of online conspiracy theories to incite real-world violence. Despite being thoroughly debunked, elements of the Pizzagate conspiracy theory continue to circulate online and have influenced other QAnon-related beliefs.
subtitle: Debunked conspiracy theory about child trafficking

claims.claim: Hillary Clinton and other Democrats ran a child sex trafficking ring.
claims.evidence: Misinterpreted emails from John Podesta's account and coded language on social media.
claims.criticism: Independent investigations and law enforcement found no evidence to support this claim. Emails were taken out of context.

claims.claim: Comet Ping Pong pizzeria was the headquarters for this trafficking ring.
claims.evidence: Proponents cited the restaurant's name, artwork, and alleged coded messages on social media.
claims.criticism: Multiple investigations revealed no evidence of any illegal activity at Comet Ping Pong. Claims about artwork and coded messages were based on misinterpretations and falsehoods.

claims.claim: The emails contained coded language referring to child abuse and trafficking.
claims.evidence: Proponents claimed that certain words and phrases were secret codes for illegal activities.
claims.criticism: Forensic linguistics experts and mainstream media outlets debunked these claims, demonstrating that the supposed codes were either misinterpretations or completely fabricated.

keyFigures.name: Jack Posobiec
keyFigures.role: Early promoter of the Pizzagate conspiracy theory on social media.

keyFigures.name: Mike Cernovich
keyFigures.role: Influential blogger and promoter of Pizzagate.

keyFigures.name: Edgar Maddison Welch
keyFigures.role: Man who fired an AR-15 rifle inside Comet Ping Pong while investigating the conspiracy.

keyFigures.name: Alex Jones
keyFigures.role: Promoted the conspiracy on his Infowars platform.

keyFigures.name: David Brock
keyFigures.role: Founder of Media Matters for America, which debunked Pizzagate.

mythologyConnections: References to ancient cults and rituals (tenuous connection claimed by some proponents)
Symbolic interpretations of pizza and food imagery (highly subjective)

timeline.event: Pizzagate conspiracy theory originates on 4chan and Reddit.
Edgar Maddison Welch fires an AR-15 rifle inside Comet Ping Pong.
Welch sentenced to four years in prison.
Pizzagate continues to circulate online and influences other conspiracy theories.

sources.author: Sheera Frenkel
